summary: This guest post by Jianyin Roachell examines China's emerging National Social Credit System, which will quantify citizens' social worth based on purchasing behavior and conduct by 2020. The author distinguishes between two components: a commercial credit system (similar to FICO) and a social management system that blacklists offenders. Rather than simply condemning the system as Orwellian, Roachell argues that cultural context matters—Confucian values have long emphasized trusting authority. The post raises concerns about how integrating credit scores into dating apps could solidify class divisions and limit social mobility, while also suggesting big data could potentially be leveraged to reduce inequality.
key_points: China's AI will likely surpass the West due to training on 1.3 billion people's data with weak privacy protections
The social credit system has two distinct parts: commercial credit (financial trustworthiness) and social management (government blacklisting)
Cultural framing: Chinese firms play 'Mother' offering rewards while government plays 'Father' monitoring bad behavior
Integration of credit scores into dating sites may entrench class divisions across generations
The author proposes 'Digital Social Mobility'—using big data to increase opportunity rather than surveillance
money_quotes: By 2020, China will roll out a national social credit system to quantify every citizen's social worth in society through what they buy and how they behave.
In China, consumers are sucking on the tit of business discounts and freemium products, while the government is monitoring bad behavior through a very data-driven censorship system.
Companies are building machine learning models that train on 1.3 Billion people's personal data points.
It has always been in China's DNA to trust and obey the authorities even in the ancient chinese imperial times.
Over time this may further solidify the classes and may even amplify the effects, where the lower-class will stay poor, and upper class will stay wealthy.

There are so many opportunities for firms and government to work together to increase the value of the people, bring them out of poverty, and give policyholders the intelligence to redistribute wealth.
key_insight: China's social credit system isn't simply dystopian surveillance—it's a complex fusion of commercial credit scoring and social control that could either entrench inequality or, if designed thoughtfully, potentially increase social mobility.
